article.title: Operation PowerOFF Seizes 53 DDoS-for-Hire Domains and Warns 75,000 Users in Largest Coordinated Takedown of Booter Services
article.summary: A 21-country law enforcement coalition dismantled dozens of DDoS-for-hire platforms and sent deterrence warnings to tens of thousands of identified users.
article.body_markdown:
## Overview

Law enforcement agencies from 21 countries conducted a coordinated enforcement week against distributed denial-of-service (DDoS)-for-hire platforms, seizing 53 domains, arresting four suspects, and sending more than 75,000 warning emails and letters to identified users of the services, according to [BleepingComputer](https://www.bleepingcomputer.com/news/security/operation-poweroff-identifies-75k-ddos-users-takes-down-53-domains/). The operation, dubbed PowerOFF and coordinated by Europol, targeted the commercial infrastructure that allows individuals to rent attack capacity against websites and online services without technical expertise.

Authorities obtained data on more than three million alleged criminal user accounts from the seized databases, per [BleepingComputer](https://www.bleepingcomputer.com/news/security/operation-poweroff-identifies-75k-ddos-users-takes-down-53-domains/). The action week, which concluded on April 16, also resulted in 25 search warrants being executed across the participating countries.

## What We Know

The operation brought together law enforcement from the United States, United Kingdom, Australia, Austria, Belgium, Brazil, Bulgaria, Denmark, Estonia, Finland, Germany, Japan, Latvia, Lithuania, Luxembourg, the Netherlands, Norway, Poland, Portugal, Sweden, and Thailand, as reported by [CyberScoop](https://cyberscoop.com/ddos-for-hire-takedowns-operation-poweroff/). Europol supported the coordination, while participating agencies dismantled booter services and seized servers, databases, and IP stressors used to conduct attacks.

DDoS-for-hire platforms, commonly known as booter or stresser services, allow users to launch cyberattacks "without needing to have any hacking skills, nor the need to run their own infrastructure," according to [TechCrunch](https://techcrunch.com/2026/04/16/european-police-email-75000-people-asking-them-to-stop-ddos-attacks/). Operators typically market these platforms as legitimate network stress-testing tools but perform no verification of whether the user owns the targeted infrastructure, per [BleepingComputer](https://www.bleepingcomputer.com/news/security/operation-poweroff-identifies-75k-ddos-users-takes-down-53-domains/).

Attacks launched through these services have typically targeted regional servers within the same continent, affecting online marketplaces, telecommunications providers, and web services, according to [CyberScoop](https://cyberscoop.com/ddos-for-hire-takedowns-operation-poweroff/). User motivations ranged from curiosity and ideological purposes to financial extortion and competitive disruption.

## Deterrence Beyond Arrests

The operation's most distinctive feature was its scale of user-facing deterrence. Beyond the four arrests and infrastructure seizures, authorities deployed a multi-layered prevention strategy. Law enforcement placed targeted search engine advertisements designed to reach younger audiences searching for DDoS-for-hire tools, attempting to divert potential first-time offenders before they entered the criminal ecosystem, per [BleepingComputer](https://www.bleepingcomputer.com/news/security/operation-poweroff-identifies-75k-ddos-users-takes-down-53-domains/).

Authorities also removed more than 100 URLs promoting illegal booter services from search engine results and attached on-chain warning messages to illicit cryptocurrency payments associated with the platforms, according to [BleepingComputer](https://www.bleepingcomputer.com/news/security/operation-poweroff-identifies-75k-ddos-users-takes-down-53-domains/). The 75,000 warning communications were sent to individuals identified through seized server data, effectively putting users on notice that their activity had been logged by law enforcement.

The DDoS threat continues to grow in scale. Cloudflare mitigated what it described as the largest DDoS attack on record in 2025, reaching 29.7 terabits per second, as reported by [TechCrunch](https://techcrunch.com/2026/04/16/european-police-email-75000-people-asking-them-to-stop-ddos-attacks/).

## What We Don't Know

Law enforcement agencies have not disclosed the identities or nationalities of the four arrested individuals, nor the specific booter platforms that were taken offline. The financial scale of the seized operations, including pricing structures and estimated revenue, has not been made public. It also remains unclear how many of the 75,000 warned users had launched actual attacks versus merely creating accounts on the platforms.

The operation follows previous PowerOFF enforcement waves, including a late 2024 takedown and arrests in Poland in May 2025, according to [CyberScoop](https://cyberscoop.com/ddos-for-hire-takedowns-operation-poweroff/). Whether this latest wave will produce a lasting reduction in available DDoS-for-hire capacity, or whether new platforms will rapidly replace the seized ones as has occurred after past takedowns, remains to be seen.
